data.title: Recession, Inflation, Or Goldilocks - What's Your Bet?
data.description:
Current Situation: Stagflation

We are currently in a stagflationary environment. The labor market is weakening, and real growth is slowing, while inflation is rising.

Based on the Fed's view, the labor market is weakening primarily due to the immigration policy. Specifically, due to the strict immigration policy, mass deportations, and voluntary departures, the labor supply is decreasing. At the same time, the demand for labor is also decreasing. Thus, the job creation is much weaker, and yet the unemployment rate remains low. Powell views this as a "low-hire low-fire market." The risk in this situation is that if the layoffs increase, the unemployment rate would likely spike quickly – and cause a recession.

Based on the Fed's view, inflation is rising primarily due to tariffs via rising prices of goods. However, this rise in inflation is likely to be only temporary, as companies gradually adjust their prices to reflect the higher cost of imports.

Thus, the Fed decided to focus more on a weakening labor market and to ignore inflation. Accordingly, it lowered the Federal fund rate by 25 bps at the September meeting and signaled two more cuts in 2025 and one additional cut in 2026 to the 3.4% level.

The financial markets are currently positioned:

  • 2-year yield (US2Y) is positioned for more aggressive cutting to 2.8% by the end of 2026.
  • 10Y yield (US10Y) has been falling, driven by the lower real yield, which signals low growth expectations, but also with steady and elevated breakeven inflation expectations at just below the critical 2.5% level, which signals that inflation remains a worry. Together, that reflects stagflation.
  • The S&P 500 (SP500) has been in a melt-up mode, supported by the idea that the Fed cuts will support the bubble, similarly to 1998.
  • Gold (GLD) continues to rise, reflecting all the risks ignored by other assets, including geopolitics.

So, what happens next?

The future path for financial assets will depend on how the current stagflationary environment evolves. There are three possible scenarios: 1) Recession, 2) Inflation, or 3) Goldilocks.

The Recession Scenario

In the recessionary scenario, the labor market continues to weaken and the unemployment rate spikes. The Fed is unable to prevent the recession with the cuts because the reasons behind the spike in the unemployment rate are not cyclical – rather they are structural or policy-based.

The Fed cuts cannot cause the increase in the labor force by bringing the immigrants back into the country or by reversing the aging of retiring baby boomers.

In this scenario, the financial markets would react as follows:

  • The Fed would be forced to cut more aggressively, which means 2Y yields would fall. However, 2Y yields are already pricing an aggressive Fed easing, which suggests that 2Y yields are already pricing a recession.
  • 10Y yields would fall toward 3% as real rates fall, and assuming that a recession causes inflation to fall, the breakeven inflation expectations would fall as well.
  • The stock market bubble would burst as earnings estimates fall and the PE valuations contract, with the sell-off similar to 2000 and 2008.

The Inflation Scenario

In the inflationary scenario, inflation becomes more persistent and potentially causes the Fed to pause the easing cycle and start increasing interest rates.

The labor market would get stronger, possibly due to the foreign direct investment (FDI) as committed to the Trump administration. There would be an increase in demand for construction workers to build factories and also for workers to work in those factories. Further, due to the labor shortage, the wages would rise, and this would trigger the price-wage inflationary spiral.

In this scenario, the financial markets would react as follows.

  • 2Y yields would rise as the Fed would be forced to get hawkish.
  • 10Y yields would rise above 5% as growth accelerates and inflation expectations increase towards the 3% level.
  • The stock market would likely have a deep correction like in 2022.
  • Gold would likely also correct as real yields increase.

The Goldilocks

The Goldilocks scenario would happen if the labor market gets stronger, growth accelerates, but inflation falls.

The labor market would get stronger, as explained in the inflationary scenario. However, in the Goldilocks case, inflation falls, as wages don't increase. This scenario could be possible if the AI boosts productivity with automatization in factories and with agentic AI for services.

In this case,

  • The Fed would be able to normalize interest rates, according to the current plans, which would push 2Y yields slightly higher.
  • The 10Y yield would stabilize at around 3.5-4%.
  • The stock market bubble could continue to inflate, although the ride could be volatile, interrupted by 10% corrections.

My Take

The current stagflation will have to evolve; either we will have a recession, higher inflation, or Goldilocks.

The market is pricing all three scenarios: 2Y yields are pricing an aggressive easing consistent with a recession, the 10Y breakeven inflation is suggesting inflation is still a worry, while the stock market is fully pricing the Goldilocks.

The key variable here is whether the foreign countries and companies will really invest in the US next year as committed. In addition, whether the expectations that the AI will boost productivity and lower inflation prove to be correct.

At this point I am skeptical about the Goldilocks. For example, the EU already suggested that it can't guarantee the $600 billion investment in the US. In my opinion, many other countries will possibly delay the committed investment and wait for the mid-terms, and the Supreme Court decision on the legality of tariffs could also be a game-changer. I am also skeptical about the AI, given the MIT study that finds that 95% of AI projects fail.

In that sense, I also view the inflationary scenario as less likely – the wages are unlikely to increase as the labor market is unlikely to bounce.

Thus, I view the recession scenario as most likely. The most important variable here is the fact that the Fed is fighting the policy events with lower interest rates – and that's likely to be ineffective. The monetary policy is effective only with cyclical events.

As a result, I see the stock market bubble burst as very likely.

Note, I do understand the Goldilocks scenario for all the bear critics, but at this point, it's all hype and hope. But we will know; let's see if the construction and manufacturing jobs pick up while the wage growth stays muted.

data.title: Monte Rosa: H1 2026 NEK7 Protein Degrader MRT-8102 Data Could Be Added POC
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Monte Rosa Therapeutics (NASDAQ:GLUE) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article I wrote titled "Monte Rosa: Novartis Deal Bolsters MGD Platform With Flush Of Cash." With respect to this article, I noted the company's prospect in advancing the molecular glue degrader of VAV1 MRT-6160 for the treatment of patients with autoimmune disorders. At that time, I also mentioned that this biotech was able to establish a large deal with Novartis (NVS) whereby they would advance this exact candidate in clinical studies. In relation to this deal, it obtained an upfront payment of $150 million along with the potential to earn up to $2.1 billion in milestone payments. There are two important updates that I want to go over this time around, which I believe further drive shareholder value.

The first update of importance to note is that it was able to establish a second large deal with Novartis. This other deal is to entail both companies developing molecular glue degraders to target immune-mediated disorders. Why is this other deal also very substantial? The reason why is because it brings forth an upfront payment of $120 million and the potential to earn up to $5.7 billion in milestone payments. More about this new deal below, but the point is that this brings forth additional cash for the company.

The second update I want to go over below in detail would be that it received IND clearance from the FDA to advance its second molecular glue degrader, MRT-8102. This NEK7-directed molecular glue degrader [MGD] is being advanced in a phase 1 single-ascending-dose [SAD] and multiple-ascending-dose [MAD] study to evaluate the use of MRT-8102 in healthy volunteers. I bring this up because it provides a major catalyst, which is that the company anticipates releasing data from this healthy volunteer study cohort in the 1st half of 2026. On top of this, there is another cohort of this phase 1 study that is going to be looking at biomarkers like C-reactive protein [CRP] and other inflammatory markers in patients with high-risk cardiovascular disease [CVD]. The point of this is to see if the drug can be moved towards targeting cardio-immunology indications such as atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease [ASCVD] and other similar disorder types.

Potential Best-In-Class NEK7 Degrader With Differentiated Profile Kicks Off In Phase 1 Testing

As I noted above, the company has seen tremendous progress in the advancement of its molecular glue degrader [MGD] MRT-8102. Before going over all of the updates relating to this program, I believe it is important to note what type of indications Monte Rosa can go after with this candidate. This is actually to be divided into specific sections of what sectors this can be used for as follows:

  • Cardio-immunology - pericarditis
  • Immuno-metabolic - MASH, Obesity and Atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease [ASCVD]
  • Rheumatology - Gout, osteoarthritis, and many others

As I briefly noted above, the main goal of the phase 1 study is to evaluate the use of MRT-8102 in terms of safety, pharmacokinetic [PK] profile and NEK7 protein degradation as well. This is to be incorporated into this study to see if such activity is achieved. However, there is a goal to also incorporate the other cohort that I was talking about, which is to look at cardiovascular disorders. This is going to be evaluated using a specific biomarker known as C-reactive protein [CRP] and with positive developments found in such a cohort, then it would be able to move on towards the targeting of large market indications like atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and pericarditis.

Atherosclerotic cardiovascular Disease [ASCVD] is characterized as the buildup of plaque on arteries in a patient, which leads to them becoming thick and hardened. The main problem with this is the blockage of blood flow, which is very bad for a patient. Especially since oxygen can't reach the intended organs to keep the body functioning properly. To make matters even worse, for patients who already have other risk factors like high cholesterol or blood pressure, their chance of getting atherosclerotic disease is increased substantially. The global atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease market is expected to reach $30.6 billion by 2035. This is just one market opportunity; the company believes that initial proof-of-concept CRP biomarker data could also possibly allow it to use this clinical candidate to target pericarditis. Pericarditis is characterized as inflammation that occurs in the pericardium of the heart, which is the two layered fluid filled sac that surrounds the heart itself. The inflammation that occurs on the pericardium is bad because it provides two important functions. One function is that it provides protection against infections for starters. Secondly, it also prevents the heart from expanding too much to cause harm. The pericarditis market, across the 7 major markets, is expected to reach $5 billion by 2035. This is another huge market opportunity if the company can get MRT-8102 to work effectively.

In order to see whether or not MRT-8102 is going to be efficient in being able to treat patients with multiple types of inflammatory disorders, it is in the process of evaluating its use in the ongoing phase 1 SAD and MAD study. This specific study is expected to recruit up to a total of 100 patients, and the intention is to randomize them to receive either MRT-8102 or a placebo. Even though this is an early-stage trial, the company is not shying away from adding in a placebo comparator component to it. That is, each cohort in this phase 1 study is going to have 6 patients taking the drug and then 2 patients given a placebo. Furthermore, it is important to note that there will be a split in how each cohort is done. The SAD portion of the study is going to have 5 dose level cohorts with a total of 40 participants, each being given a once-daily oral dose of MRT-8102. Then, with respect to the MAD portion of this early-stage trial, there will be a total of 3 dose levels of 24 patients receiving 7 daily doses of drug over a period of time.

The most important aspect of early-stage studies such as this would be safety. Thus, first and foremost, it is important if the drug ends up being tolerable. From there, it goes to having a little bit of efficacy or mechanism of action [MOA] proven. Since MRT-8102 is an MGD, the goal is to prove that this candidate can degrade the protein NEK7. What's the point of small molecule degradation of this protein? The reason why is because this protein is responsible for putting into motion NLRP3 inflammasome activation that drives inflammation in these patients. Another mode of action that it accomplishes is to block downstream release of IL-1β and IL-18 pro-inflammatory cytokines. How does this happen? Well, with no protein of NEK7 in place, macrophages present can't establish sufficient cytokine release, and thus, the goal of MRT-8102 is to block this action and, in turn, reduce inflammatory activity that these disorders cause. The whole purpose of me bringing up this program now that Monte Rosa is working on it is because it is not going to be long for investors to get an early glimpse at data for this program. As a matter of fact, it believes that it is going to be able to release safety, PK, and NEK7 protein degradation results in the 1st half of 2026.

As far as an expansion opportunity goes, this leads to another important aspect of this phase 1 SAD/MAD study using MRT-8102 in the targeting of healthy volunteers. This deals with Part 3 of it, which is going to include a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled portion of cardiovascular disorder risk patients. Specifically, the point here is to recruit patients who are both obese and also have elevated levels of C-reactive protein [CRP]. What's the point of this? Well, this biomarker measures inflammation present in a patient and also predicts the risk of the patient having a heart attack or stroke. This is established when a patient is said to have high levels of high-sensitivity CRP. Thus, the goal is to see if MRT-8102 can have an impact on this specific biomarker. If this finding occurs in the exploration of Part 3 of this Phase 1 SAD/MAD study, then the company has a good shot at moving forward towards the use of this MGD candidate towards the targeting of ASCVD and pericarditis.

Second Novartis Agreement Further Establishes Use Of Molecular Glue Degraders

As I laid out above, Monte Rosa Therapeutics was able to initiate its phase 1 SAD/MAD study in healthy volunteers and the other cohort targeting patients with increased cardiovascular disease risk due to obesity and elevated levels of CRP with its candidate MRT-8102. This is a program that it fully owns, but it also is in the process of advancing its lead molecular glue degrader MRT-6160 in the targeting of patients with immune-mediated disorders with its partner Novartis. Speaking of which, both companies are in the process of evaluating this candidate in soon-to-be initiated phase 2 studies. This was the first deal established and it was a large one in and of itself. This entailed an upfront $150 million payment, along with the ability to earn up to $2.1 billion in development, regulatory, and sales-based milestones.

It seems that Novartis is happy with this partnership, and the reason why I state this is because not only is MRT-6160 being advanced into phase 2 clinical testing, but the big pharma came back for another deal relating to Monte Rosa's MGDs technology. The goal of this separate, newly established collaboration agreement is to develop MGDs specifically for immune-mediated diseases. With that being said, with this new agreement, Novartis gains access to one undisclosed discovery target for starters. In addition, they are also being given the option to license two other programs as part of Monte Rosa's preclinical immunology pipeline. The good news here is that it provided another influx of cash, and the company could be eligible to potentially earn another batch of milestone payments as well. As part of this signed deal, it received an upfront payment of $120 million and could be eligible to earn up to an additional $5.7 billion in milestone payments.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Monte Rosa Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash, and marketable securities of $295.5 million as of June 30, 2025. The company is in excellent shape in terms of cash, and this is because of the collaboration deals it has been able to make. Two of such collaboration deals, which I went over above, happened with Novartis. I didn't even go over another deal that this biotech had made with another big pharma by the name of Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY). This was an older deal that was established back in 2023, but brought in an upfront payment of $50 million. Plus, the ability to earn up to $2 billion in milestone payments.

With that being said, the reason for the huge cash runway would be all of such deals it has made to date. It notes in the release of its Q2 2025 financial results and business update that it believes its cash on hand will be enough to fund its operations into 2028. In the event that it does need additional cash, it already has an Open Market Sale Agreement with Jefferies LLC. In place if it chooses to utilize it. An SEC Form S-3 was declared effective in March of 2025, and this was the basis for it being able to add an amendment to this with Jefferies, where it could offer and sell up to $150 million worth of its common stock from time to time. The company burns roughly $38.8 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $30.7 million in R&D expenses and then $8.1 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Monte Rosa Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the partnership that this company has with Novartis for the development of MRT-6160 for the treatment of patients with immune-mediated disorders. The companies are looking to advance this candidate forward in further establishing proof-of-concept in VAV1 degradation with this candidate in phase 2 studies. The risk here is that even though the phase 1 SAD/MAD portions of the study showed >90% VAV1 degradation in peripheral blood T-cells [along with peripheral blood B-cells], there is no guarantee that this effect is going to be shown in a sustained function in the next expected studies. In addition, the SAD portion saw the effect of inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokines of IL-2, IFN-γ, and IL-17A in peripheral whole blood T-cells. There is no assurance that such inhibition of cytokines is going to be shown in the mid-stage trials, which again are expected to start soon enough.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the development of MRT-8102 as an MGD of the NEK7 protein. The company was able to initiate a phase 1 study using this drug candidate in healthy volunteer patients as part of SAD and MAD cohorts. The point of this study is to establish the safety of MRT-8102, along with the MOA to achieve NEK7 protein degradation. The risk here is that even though other MGDs like MRT-6160 were shown to be safe/tolerable in early-stage testing, there is no assurance that this will be the case with this other clinical candidate for starters. Secondly, the point is to achieve protein degradation of NEK7 to prevent assembly of NLRP3 inflammasome activity and then subsequent releases of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and interleukin-18 [IL-18]. The second risk is that there is no assurance that MRT-8102 will achieve the desired protein degradation outcome of NEK7 to prevent inflammation in a variety of inflammatory disorders.

The third risk to consider would still relate to the phase 1 study of MGD MRT-8102 in the phase 1 SAD/MAD study of healthy volunteer patients. Specifically, with part 3 of this trial, which incorporated a separate cohort to look at patients with increased cardiovascular disease [CVD] risk in obesity patients and as such, who have elevated levels of C-reactive protein [CRP]. The risk here is that there is no assurance that MRT-8102 is going to be able to significantly reduce CRP levels in order to continue to be evaluated in further studies, for starters. Nor that the company is going to be able to use this candidate towards the targeting of other cardiovascular disorders.

The fourth and final risk to consider would be the development of another MGD I didn't discuss in detail. This would be in terms of MRT-2359, which is being evaluated in a phase 1/2 study to target solid tumors with L-MYC or N-MYC amplification. While this study was made to look at all solid tumor types, the company is solely focused on MYC-driven tumors of castration-resistant prostate cancer [CRPC] patients. The company anticipates releasing additional results from this phase 1/2 study in the 2nd half of 2025. The risk here is that a lack of an efficacy signal would not only mean the halting of this program, but also the removal of expanding to another batch of 20 to 30 patients. In addition, the hope is to expand MRT-2359 to target hormone receptor-positive [HR+] breast cancer patients as well.

Conclusion

Monte Rosa Therapeutics has been able to continue to advance the development of its MGD MRT-6061 with its partner Novartis. This is evidenced by the fact that both companies were able to show positive data from a phase 1 SAD/MAD study in healthy volunteers. This specific data set is what allowed the decision to move towards the soon-to-be-initiated phase 2 studies and the broad targeting of immune-mediated disorders. In this article, I specifically went over the ability to advance another MGD from its pipeline known as MRT-8102. The initial goal is to evaluate this drug for indications like ASCVD and pericarditis.

However, should the development of this drug go well, the company could expand its use in targeting other large target market indications like MASH, obesity, and osteoarthritis. I would say the best part of all about this biotech is its ability to obtain cash through collaborations it had developed. With the most recent upfront payment of $120 million being obtained with a second separate deal with Novartis to develop other MGDs, besides the deal already achieved with respect to MRT-6160. With initial safety/PK/NEK7 protein degradation data from the phase 1 SAD/MAD study of MRT-8102 in healthy volunteers expected in the 1st half of 2026, plus a new second deal established with Novartis to advance other preclinical MGDs, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Market May Be About To Be Turned Upside Down
data.description:
Yesterday, the Federal Reserve finally resumed cutting interest rates, albeit with a fairly timid 25-basis-point cut. Moreover, the dot plot shifted from the last meeting to reflect expectations of lower federal funds rates in the coming quarters and years than were previously forecast. However, a certain sector of the market behaved abnormally in the wake of the shifting expectations for interest rates. In this article I will detail why I think the market is about to get turned upside down.

Why Rate Cuts Usually Push Long-Term Rates Lower

Traditionally, when the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates, it carries over to the long end of the curve, bringing down long-term interest rates as well, thereby making bond (BND) prices rise, along with interest-rate-sensitive bond proxy equities such as utilities (XLU), infrastructure (UTF), and REITs (VNQ), as well as fixed income products like preferreds (PFFA). The reason for this is simply that there is typically a length premium for bonds such that in a normal yield curve scenario, long-term interest rates are higher than short-term interest rates by a certain amount. Therefore, when short-term interest rates fall, it creates room for long-term interest rates to fall as well.

Moreover, the conditions that tend to drive the Fed to cut interest rates—namely weak inflation and/or a weakening economy—also tend to lend themselves toward lower long-term interest rates, since lower inflation means that an investor feels more confident in locking up capital for a long period of time with a lower return, as it needs less of a premium in its yield to offset the corrosive effects of inflation. Additionally, a weakening economy makes bonds an attractive safe haven, since investors would rather have a contractual and secured rate of return on their capital than risk equities in an environment where the economy is shrinking and bankruptcies are rising.

Why Long-Term Rates Rose Instead

However, after the Fed announced its rate cut today and reduced outlook for the federal funds rate for the foreseeable future via adjustments to the dot plot, long-term interest rates actually ended up rising after some short-term volatility. Moreover, this carried over to interest-rate-sensitive securities like REITs, which ended the day in the red and materially lower than they were before the Federal Reserve’s announcement.

Why is this, given what I just mentioned about how lower short-term interest rates often correlate with and arguably even cause lower long-term interest rates? The reason is simply that this Federal Reserve rate cut was done at a time that was not ideal for a cut based on the data and the Fed’s dual mandate. Inflation remains about 100 basis points above the Fed’s 2% target rate based on the most recent CPI reading. Core CPI was even a bit more than 100 basis points above the Fed’s target rate.

The Fed’s Priorities And Political Pressure

What this means is that the Fed is prioritizing other factors ahead of fighting inflation right now, including a weaker-than-expected labor market due to a combination of weak jobs performance in recent months as well as massive downward revisions of jobs under the previous administration. Moreover, consumer confidence is declining, household delinquencies are rising, and consumer debt across numerous categories is near or at all-time highs.

Those factors, combined with significant political pressure from the current administration as well as the need to try to reduce pressure on the nation’s ballooning budget deficit, of which interest on the federal debt is becoming an increasingly large portion, pushed Chairman Powell and company to cut interest rates and signal for several more to come.

Why Long-Term Yields Face Upward Pressure

However, with inflation at an elevated level and no sign of either party being truly serious about cutting spending in any material manner, it appears that there remains a stronger case for higher long-term interest rates than for lower long-term interest rates. In fact, given that inflation remains a problem and the budget deficit does not appear likely to shrink for the foreseeable future, between Treasury (TLT) issuances continuing to flood the bond market and continued investor concerns about inflation, which could be aggravated further by the recent rate cut, there is quite a bit of upward pressure on long-term bond yields right now.

Why The Market May Get Turned Upside Down

So why do I think this is going to make the market turn upside down? First of all, it is going to be a bitter pill to swallow for long-suffering REIT investors who have seen the sector lag the broader market by a wide margin ever since the Fed began raising rates in 2022.

The hope was that once the Fed pivoted to rate cuts, it would undo the headwind that had built up over the past several years from rising interest rates.

However, the problem is that most REITs are more impacted by long-term interest rates since their cash flows tend to be long-dated in nature, especially with triple-net lease REITs like Realty Income (O). They also tend to take out the vast majority of their debt through long-term bond and mortgage issuances. Therefore, this rate cut appears unlikely to provide much meaningful relief for them, given that there remain significant factors in place to push long-term interest rates higher, or at the very least, keep them near current levels.

Moreover, for the broader market (NYSEARCA:SPY), this cut is also going to be problematic because, first, it could drive inflation higher, which would likely weigh on many companies that have already seen their margins pinched from inflationary pressures, even as the increased tariffs in August bite further into profit margins. Second, if long-term interest rates do indeed remain higher for longer—or worse, continue to climb—it could puncture market hopes for lower rates that may have been at least partly responsible for the strong recovery in equities this year following the initial sell-off in early April.

Given that, based on numerous valuation models, the market currently trades in very overvalued territory, and investors have already bet heavily on AI generating significant future profits—even if not for quite some time—a further increase in interest rates on the long end of the curve will only increase the pressure on AI to deliver sooner than ever before in order to adequately compensate investors for the risk premium relative to a rising yield on long-term interest rates.

As such, if this ends up taking place and the market realizes that the Fed can no longer remain in control of long-term interest rates as the bond market revolts against runaway deficit spending by the federal government, as well as the Fed’s failure to adequately tame inflation, it could cause a major market pullback. This would be contrary to the expectations that rate cuts prop up the market, as they often have in the past.

Positioning My Portfolio

Given this somewhat uncertain or even negative outlook for the markets, how am I positioning my portfolio? First of all, I am remaining meaningfully underweight pure bond-proxy type investments such as triple-net lease REITs in my total return-focused portfolios. For my income-focused retirement portfolio, I am still able to have some triple net lease REIT exposure simply because it provides a defensive and attractive yield, and I have been able to obtain that exposure on a very attractive value basis.

However, in my total return-focused portfolios, I am particularly focused on sectors that will hold up well during stagflation and have limited dependence on the long-term bond market, thereby helping insulate myself from a higher-for-longer interest rate environment as well as a potentially weaker economic growth and higher inflation environment.

Some picks that I particularly like right now include precious metals bullion ETFs (SLV)(GLD), bullion CEFs (CEF), and yield generating bullion leases, as well as inflation-resistant REITs like Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA), which also has a very strong balance sheet with low leverage that should help make it quite resilient to rising long-term interest rates. The essential nature of its business (multi-family real estate) should also help it hold up fairly well in a weak economic environment.

I also like inflation-indexed infrastructure like ONEOK (OKE), which trades at a highly compelling valuation, has inflation-indexed long-duration contracts, and a strong balance sheet with significant free cash flow generation that it is using to pay down debt aggressively, thereby further reducing its dependence on the bond market and exposure to rising long-term interest rates.

Investor Takeaway

While traditionally, Federal Reserve rate cuts provide a powerful tailwind to equity and bond markets, this time the market's behavior appears poised to be turned upside down. This is due to several macro factors, in particular the runaway deficit spending of the federal government as well as the persistently high inflation that the U.S. economy is battling. As a result, I think it is a difficult environment for overpriced equities as well as bond-proxy securities that are heavily dependent on long-term debt refinancings. Therefore, I am positioning my portfolio increasingly toward stagflation-resistant investments that have very strong balance sheets at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Gladstone Investment: Most BDCs Will Cut, But Not This Monthly Yielder
data.description:
I have been bullish on Gladstone Investment (NASDAQ:GAIN) all the way since December 2023, when I issued my first article on the stock, titled: Gladstone Investment: Why This Monthly 10% Yielder Is A Buy.

Since then, GAIN has not only delivered attractive absolute returns, but also outperformed the business development company, or BDC, market (BIZD):

Most of the returns have been accommodated by the yield-component, which at least theoretically should not imply too pronounced overvaluation now. Yet, if we look at the current yield, we will notice that GAIN is offering ~6.8% in dividend, which is meaningfully below the ~10% level when I circulated my original piece. However, the key reason for this difference is not registered price gains, but instead a reduction in supplemental distributions - i.e., a completely understandable thing given the prevailing conditions in the BDC sector.

Having said that, if we want to buy GAIN today, we have to pay a slight premium of around 8%. While this might not sound like too big a deal, in the context of how other peers are priced, we are indeed talking about a rather rich multiple. The BDC sector median P/NAV is 0.90x.

Let me now share my updated views on GAIN.

Thesis review

I will immediately start by saying that I remain bullish on GAIN despite the optically unfavorable valuation setting. Another aspect that might surprise some of my followers is that GAIN's base dividend coverage is exactly at 100%. This means that, theoretically, GAIN has no margin of safety in the system to weather the forthcoming storms from, say, lower base rates and potentially rising corporate bankruptcies.

However, if we peel back the onion a bit, we will quickly recognize that it is completely logical and rational to pay a premium price for GAIN. In other words, the price is justified by the underlying fundamentals, and, importantly, the dividend could be deemed sustainable.

I will give you three key reasons for this.

First, GAIN has a differentiated approach towards private credit lending. It is effectively a turnkey provided of equity and debt with a target mix of ~25% equity and 75% debt on a single transaction level. Traditionally, BDCs either do not provide equity at all or do so but in a limited fashion. Think here of Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (NYSE:MSDL), Blackstone Secured Lending (NYSE:BXSL) and Oaktree Specialty Lending (NASDAQ:OCSL).

Currently, about 37% of GAIN’s portfolio is comprised of equity products, which is just massive and certainly one of the highest shares in the entire BDC sector. And here, I can give you two important messages:

  1. Even with two-thirds of the portfolio located outside high-yield private credit investments, GAIN is able to cover the dividend. This is possible because many of GAIN's equity investments produce cash (i.e., pay dividends), and the remaining chunk of portfolio is underwritten at quite rich yields (~13%).
  2. More importantly, since the equity investments are structured from the start and backed by strong cash flows, GAIN is in an excellent spot to actually benefit from the lower base rates. In the lower base rate environments, the M&A markets tend to open up, creating more fruitful conditions for GAIN to monetize the existing equity stakes, which could bring in negatively correlated returns to falling loan income streams.

Second, from the financial risk perspective, GAIN is also strongly positioned. As of Q2, 2025, GAIN had a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.06x, which is below the sector average of 1.20x. This is actually another point of positive surprise. It is not that often that overcapitalized BDCs with notable exposure to equity products can cover their base dividends with the NII generation levels. Plus, the conservative capital structure indicates that GAIN definitely has an embedded potential to expand the portfolio base further, which is certainly positive to the NII per share generation and NAV stability. This is what GAIN's historical track record looks like (including an indication of Q1, 2026 development, which corresponds to Q2, 2025 calendar year).

Third, you can correct me if I am wrong, but, to be honest, I have not noticed another BDC except for GAIN that has absolutely no PIK income. The absence of PIK income implies that the base dividend coverage is supported by pure cash. For example, such peers as Golub Capital BDC (NASDAQ:GBDC), Nuveen Churchill Direct Lending Corp. (NYSE:NCDL) and Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC) with base dividend coverage levels of ~100% have actually deficit cash dividend funding situations due to the non-cash producing PIK streams. So, in order to close this gap, they have to tap into credit facilities or simply utilize loan principal repayment cash inflows that ultimately impair NAV and/or capital structures.

The bottom line

All in all, paying a premium of ~8% is acceptable here. In my view, the key competitive advantage of GAIN is its exposure to high-quality equity investments at a notable scale. This forms a similar equity story as for Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN) and Fidus Investment (NASDAQ:FDUS), which play in the lower middle market space, focusing also on already strongly cash-flowing company equity participation - just like GAIN. But in GAIN's case, the equity bias is even more pronounced, which comes at the expense of smaller income streams. Yet, given that they are still nicely backed by pure cash generation and there is an embedded firepower to grow the book, my expectation is that we will see a continued stability in the coverage despite lower base rates.

In a nutshell, GAIN has paid ~240 consecutive monthly distributions to its shareholders, and this time will, in my view, not be different.

So, I would recommend conservative BDC investors to consider GAIN for their BDC portfolio income strengthening and overall diversification purposes.

data.title: Amazon's Growth Spurt Is Heating Up
data.description:
As a long-term holder, I wouldn’t blame you for calling me biased… but I still see Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) as one of the most compelling long-term investment opportunities today, despite having what might seem like a “rich” valuation.

At the moment, AMZN stock trades at ~$231 per share and is up nearly 24% over the last year and 54% from five years ago. Today, I view Amazon as being in the early stages of several new and exciting growth catalysts that I believe will continue to reward long-term investors.

In this analysis, I’ll defend and reaffirm my Strong Buy rating on Amazon.

AI That's Already Generating Revenue

What I consider the most immediate potential growth driver is Amazon’s AI comeback through Alexa+ and AWS Bedrock.

The company has recently introduced Alexa+, with a pricing twist. They’re charging about $20 per month for non-Prime members, while it’s completely free for Prime members. But why would Amazon give away something they could charge for? Because it strengthens the Prime ecosystem as it’s worth more than the immediate subscription revenue.

This also means a much stronger moat around the membership, which already has over 200 million subscribers globally. With this reach, nurturing the existing customer base makes more sense than acquiring new ones.

The new Alexa+ is embedded with what Amazon calls agentic capabilities. So it’s no longer the same old device that you can ask to play music or check the weather. With agentic features, Alexa+ is said to be capable of completing specific tasks on your behalf, such as booking flights or adding schedules to your calendar, orchestrating thousands of services and devices to meet your needs.

On the investment front, Amazon has poured $8 billion into Anthropic, one of the leading AI research companies, which gives Amazon a significant edge in AI technology. And with a planned $100 billion CapEx for 2025, the company’s been investing heavily in AI. Should this move in the right direction, Amazon could be the next major computing platform.

That said, I believe many investors are missing the true potential of this investment. Amazon is essentially building the infrastructure for the entire AI economy, and when businesses require computing power, where do you think they’ll turn to? Computing platforms. AWS, for example, is already seeing this benefit, with AWS segment revenue increasing 17.5% to $30.9 billion in Q2 ‘25, thanks to AI workload adoption.

AWS Bedrock also deserves attention, as it gives investors an idea of how Amazon is approaching enterprise AI. Bedrock provides access to multiple foundation models, such as Amazon’s own Nova and models from Anthropic, which offer a much more flexible approach than forcing clients to use a single AI model. An LLM that’s great at writing code might not be the best for analyzing financial data.

Project Kuiper Satellite Opportunity

Project Kuiper, its proposed satellite network constellation, I think is perhaps Amazon’s most ambitious project yet. When you think of such a business model, there’s one company that comes to mind: SpaceX’s Starlink. Today, Amazon is quietly building its own low-orbit network, and it could become the next multi-billion-dollar business as global connectivity catches up to advancements. Nevertheless, the project seems to be under the radar of many investors.

Amazon aims to deploy 3,236 satellites in more than 80 launches, with a full-scale deployment that began in April 2025. According to recent data, Amazon has sent over 100 satellites into orbit and expects more than 200 by the end of the year, which means the execution appears ahead of schedule. Once the first 578 satellites are deployed, commercialization will commence, which could happen as soon as mid-2026, given the current deployment rate.

At the same time, Bank of America projected that Project Kuiper’s revenue would reach $7.1 billion by 2032, assuming a 30% market share. I think that’s rather conservative when considering the potential multiple revenue streams available. It’s no longer an internet connection for everyday consumers like us—Kuiper can be used in maritime settings, where vessels constantly need internet connectivity, or in airline settings, where passenger Wi-Fi has become a necessity.

Speaking of which, JetBlue has already signed up as the first airline customer of Project Kuiper just this month, with services expected to begin in 2027, and that’s just the beginning.

Another facet that caught my attention about Kuiper is its integration with AWS. Essentially, Amazon will offer private Internet connectivity into AWS without routing through a public network. It may not be a big deal for everyday users like us, but enterprise customers who are concerned about security and performance could significantly benefit from this approach.

For instance, some Australian mines are utilizing driverless trains to haul tons of minerals from remote locations, and these trains are completely autonomous and powered by AI. With Project Kuiper, mining operations in remote Australia can connect directly to AWS servers without their data ever passing through public networks.

On the consumer front, Amazon aims to produce terminals for less than $400, which tells me that its price will be set a little above that. If that is the case, the competitive pricing would make satellite internet accessible to millions of households currently underserved by traditional services. They will most likely lose money on each terminal initially, but sacrificing long-term gains to achieve long-term dominance isn’t an uncommon approach with Amazon.

And although it’s ever-present in most businesses, some worry about potential regulatory issues; however, I think the regulatory requirements work in Amazon’s favor. To maintain its FCC license, the company must launch more than half of its satellites by July 2026 and all satellites by July 2029.

The tight timelines certainly create unimaginable pressure for deployment, but on the bright side, they also serve as a tall barrier to entry for potential competitors. I mean, not many companies have the financial muscle to launch thousands of low-orbit satellites in such a short period of time.

Advertising Business

Amazon’s advertising business is also growing faster than the overall revenue numbers while generating margins that most businesses can only dream of. In Q2 ‘25, advertising revenue rose 22% to reach ~$15.7 billion. Ad margins aren’t reported, but one could easily expect it to be easily above 60-70%.

But why is Amazon’s advertising platform so valuable? Well, it comes down to data… but not just any data. You see, Amazon knows what you actually buy, not just what you browse or search for. This direct purchase data is gold for advertisers because it removes the costliest variable from advertising—the guesswork.

If you’re selling premium coffee, you should show your products to people who actually buy premium coffee, not to those who might be interested based on their search history.

Ads on Prime Video are another growth driver for Amazon. Again, there are over 200 million Prime members globally, all of whom are willing to pay for premium services. This is exactly the audience that advertisers are dreaming of.

Unlike traditional TV ads, where advertisers are throwing everything at the wall to see if anything sticks, Amazon can track how many viewers saw their ads and went on to make a purchase.

Moreover, the profit margins on advertising have surpassed those of traditional retail. Selling physical products generates single-digit margins at best after accounting for warehousing, shipping, and in many cases, returns. Advertising, on the other hand, can create margins exceeding 50% since there’s virtually no incremental cost to serving an ad.

However, I believe the real moat lies in the current regulatory environment. As privacy laws become stricter and third-party cookies disappear, first-party data becomes more valuable, which is what Amazon has in abundance. They don’t need to track consumers across the internet to find their data since they’re already shopping on Amazon's platform.

Financial Performance

All these growth initiatives need to translate into actual financial performance, so let’s see what kind of picture the numbers paint.

Compared to the same period last year, Amazon’s Q2 ‘25 revenue rose 13% to $167.70 billion. This growth might not appear spectacular by any means compared to smaller tech companies, but double-digit growth from such a massive base is remarkable.

Gross profit also increased 17% to $86.90 billion. This suggests that Amazon is successfully mixing their higher-margin business with traditional segments. The advertising business I just mentioned is one example of this shift in mix.

Meanwhile, operating income jumped ~31% to $19.17 billion, with margins expanding from 9.9% to 11.4%. Considering the company’s aggressive investment in AI infrastructure and satellite deployment, this margin expansion is truly impressive. The fact that there’s cash to fund all these massive initiatives also tells us that the core business is more profitable than these numbers indicate.

As a result of these double-digit improvements, Amazon’s net income for Q2 ’25 surged ~35% to $18.16 billion, which translates to basic EPS of $1.71 (notable increase from $1.29 of the same period last year).

So why is this crucial? When you see earnings growing nearly 3x faster than revenue, it often translates to better operating leverage, which usually only happens when a business reaches a scale where it can spread fixed costs over a larger revenue base.

Stock Performance

At the time of this publication, AMZN stock trades at ~$231 per share, up 5.57% YTD, 23.94% over the past year, and it has gained 53.97% over the last five years. That said, the company’s forward P/E ratio (GAAP) is 35.39x, which is over 76% higher than the sector median of 20.10x, and many are concerned.

But remember: Amazon isn’t a retailer or even a cloud computing company at this point. They’re a collection of ventures that are each capable of generating hundreds of billions in revenue. Project Kuiper alone could be worth the premium if execution goes according to plan.

Considering what I’m buying, I believe the premium is well-justified.

Risk: AWS Challenge

Of course, every investment comes with some risk. In Amazon’s case, it’s the potential softening of demand for AWS. Specifically, AWS margins dropped from 35.5% in Q2 ‘24 to 32.9% in Q2 ‘25, and that’s not a good indication. Moreover, AWS's 18% growth also lags behind Azure’s 34% and Google Cloud’s 32%.

Amazon’s TTM free cash flow also declined to $18.2 billion, compared to the $53 billion of the same period last year, which is primarily driven by the massive investments I previously discussed. Put simply, Amazon is spending nearly a year’s worth of AWS revenue on AI investments, which I think could be an unprecedented bet on future demands.

Sure, I’m an Amazon bull by any and all means, but if AI workloads don’t materialize as expected, or if pricing pressure from competitors prevents adequate returns, the value of these investments and growth development in the pipelines could diminish quickly.

But personally, I don’t believe that AWS's margin compression will lead to a downward spiral but more of a temporary consolidation. I mean, the company has a long history of investing heavily in new opportunities before they start paying dividends—they even have a robotics division, which I could discuss in a separate analysis.

Back in the day, industry “experts” also criticized Amazon for building fulfillment centers that hammered the margins, but those investments now serve as a moat that makes their retail dominance nearly impossible to topple today. I also see the recent AI investment following a similar pattern, but of course, the question about the potential materialization of demand remains.

As for the spending levels, I personally believe that this is necessary to maintain a competitive position in what could be the most important tech market of the next decade, which is artificial intelligence. I also think that for Amazon’s caliber, underinvesting and losing AI leadership would be far more damaging for its long-term investors than the current margin pressure, so I’m overly concerned about it.

Investment Verdict

So, where does all this leave us as small investors? I think Amazon offers a combination of growth and stability, and that the risk-reward profile favors buyers at current levels.

Sure, there will be volatility as the company goes through this growth phase and intense competition, but for those who can look past quarterly headwinds, I believe Amazon is one of the most favorable long-term opportunities in the market today.

Considering all the reasons mentioned above, including the potential risk, my Strong Buy rating for Amazon remains unchanged.

data.title: Goldman Sachs Small Cap Growth Insights Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

US Small Cap Growth equities (Russell 2000 Growth Index) rose 11.97% in the second quarter of 2025, outperforming the Value Index by over 7%. Growth stocks rebounded sharply after a weak first quarter, supported by easing inflation, a pickup in risk appetite, and strong earnings. After a volatile April marked by tariff concerns, markets stabilized and rallied through May and June. Growth’s year-to-date decline narrowed to just 0.48% by quarter-end, outperforming its value counterparts both in the quarter, and the year-to-date period. Information Technology and Communication Services were the best performing sectors over the quarter.

Performance Review

The GS Small Cap Growth Insights Fund (I-share, net) returned 12.41% in the second quarter of 2025, outperforming its benchmark, the Russell 2000 Growth Index, by 45 basis points. Although challenged in June, the positive performance during May extended the Fund’s strong year-to-date performance; so far this year, the Fund has outperformed its benchmark by 63 basis points (I-share, net).

Our Themes and Trends pillar was the largest contributor to the Fund’s relative performance over 2Q25. Gains were concentrated during the month of May. Our proprietary signals focused on identifying economic linkages and complex connections between companies contributed the most, particularly within the Industrials sector.

Our Fundamental Mispricings pillar was additive to the Fund’s excess return during the second quarter of the year. Our industry rotation signals contributed positively during the period, particularly across our overweight positions within the Industrials sector. This helped offset headwinds in our relative valuation signals within the Health Care sector.

Our Sentiment Analysis pillar also contributed to excess returns during the second quarter of 2025. Despite headwinds in June, strong performance in May resulted in positive excess returns. Our suites of signals that seek to gauge investor sentiment as well as events were both additive particularly in the Industrials sector.

Our High-Quality Business Models pillar detracted slightly over the quarter. Gains in May were offset by headwinds in June. Challenges at the end of the quarter primarily came from our signals evaluating the quality of company’s management. Most losses occurred within the Industrials and Health Care sectors.
