data.title: Amazon: It's Never Too Late To Buy
data.description:
Updating My Thesis On AMZN

My most recent article on Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) stock came out in mid-July, and it was a preview piece on the then-upcoming fiscal Q2 print, which I thought should have led to AMZN's rally continuation if the firm beat. It happened eventually - Amazon beat the consensus for Q2. But we've been seeing Amazon's relative lagging compared to the S&P 500 index (SPY) (SP500) since the earnings release date.

I think we can't blame the recent Q2 results themselves, as they didn't disappoint - AMZN's correction is likely just a temporary thing that should eventually smooth out over time. This is my base case assumption regarding Amazon, so this is where my updated title comes from - it's never too late to buy AMZN.

Although the stock might indeed have quite high valuation multiples right now at first glance, I think this premium is reasonable and it's there to stay for the foreseeable future, thanks to the existing moat and ever-growing business verticals, especially when we're talking about the AI-related verticals within AWS. So, like in the past few cases, I'm reiterating my long-term "Buy" rating on AMZN.

Why Do I Think So?

Amazon's released quarterly results were once again better than expected on all fronts, as far as I see it. Their consolidated quarterly sales of ~$167.7 billion (+13.3% YoY) beat the consensus estimate by over $5.6 billion or 3.46% (that's the strongest beating momentum in recent years, based on Seeking Alpha data). And their adjusted EPS came in at ~$1.68 (+33.3% YoY), beating the consensus by a massive 27.19%, according to Seeking Alpha. The magnitude of their double-beat wasn't just a one-off event for Amazon. And the management seemed quite confident on the post-Q2 call. Otherwise, we wouldn't see their Q3 revenue guidance climbing to the range of $174-179.5 billion, which obviously triggered a widespread Wall Street analysts' reaction (see the earnings revisions).

Few analysts expected the level of Amazon's core retail comeback in Q2, but it actually happened. The firm reported that this year's Prime Day set a few historical records for sales, items sold, and new Prime sign-ups, and what was even more importantly, in my view, this year's Prime Day proved that Amazon can greatly leverage its existing ecosystem in such times.

The margins were higher than most analysts were pricing in initially, and the "efficiency flywheel" that we've been hearing about for the past few earnings calls is still leading to tangible gains on the bottom line. According to the updated commentary of the Q2 call, by increasing the use of direct shipping lanes, Amazon effectively "reduced the average distance packages travel by ~12%" and lowered handling touches per unit by ~15%, so their EBIT margins (in both NA and International segments) are higher and more sustainable now versus the industry's norms. And while being bigger in absolute terms, Amazon keeps gaining a massive competitive advantage on this incremental "efficiency, efficiency", which is impossible to beat to date, in my opinion.

What Amazon proved in Q2 was the fact that it can largely mitigate the tariff impact that everyone on the Street was concerned about lately. The diversity of Amazon's third-party marketplace has given it a well-managed buffer that basically absorbed cost pressures, and it did so way more effectively than its smaller peers. Going forward, I see no major hits to this competitive advantage any time soon.

If costs end up being higher, who will absorb them, but what we can share is what we've seen thus far, which is that through the first half of the year, we haven't yet seen diminishing demand nor price is meaningfully appreciating. We also have such diversity of sellers in our marketplace over 2 million sellers in total with different strategies of whether to pass on higher cost to consumers, the customers are advantaged shopping at Amazon because they're more likely to find lower prices on the items they care about.

Source: AMZN's Q2 earnings call.

When looking at AMZN's forward P/E of over 30x, one can think that the stock has become pricier compared to the past few years. But I don't quite agree with that.

The business segment that is mostly responsible for the valuation premium in Amazon's stock price - the firm's AWS segment - has grown its top line by 17.5% YoY to a massive $123 billion ARR. While I agree with skeptics saying that there are smaller peers growing faster, the ecosystem of multiple AI-related layers that AWS has grown into is what makes a difference. It's not just a cloud provider anymore.

With the new Trainium2 chip at the bottom layer of the stack and Amazon's eventual reliance reduction on third-party suppliers in the future, the firm can a) move faster in innovations like Strands and AgentCore to create and deploy AI agents securely at scale, and b) let them monetize the new offerings quicker and to a higher extent than some investors may assume today. I don't know about this catalyst's timing, but once the Street realizes this potential, I think this should give AMZN investors more hope for valuation premium sustainability.

We see that AWS's EBIT margin dipped from the previous Q1 peaks, but it was largely expected, and I'm now looking forward to seeing the previous AI-driven CapEx translate into even higher efficiency and new marginality peaks next year.

By the way, speaking about AMZN's exact valuation levels, I noted above that the statement of higher-than-before multiples is only partially true. In reality, we see that the multiples are becoming reasonable now - at least on a log scale, we see Amazon stock trading close to its historical bottom in terms of price-to-earnings ratios:

The premium in valuation still exists, and we can't call AMZN a cheap stock when we simply use conventional metrics such as TTM EV/EBITDA or P/E ratios and compare them with the industry norms. Amazon is not a "normal company" in this sense, which is why Seeking Alpha's quant system shows a "D-" grade for the stock's valuation.

But I see "reasonableness" in the way the market prices the stock to date in the firm's future operational scaling. We can see it clearly when we take a look at Amazon's 2-year forward EV/EBITDA ratio, which is now only at 10.9x, and it's over 3x less than the long-term average for Amazon, based on YCharts:

So, the growth factor that explains the "reasonableness" of AMZN's valuation doesn't go anywhere. Over the next 5 years, AMZN should grow its EPS with a CAGR of over 19% annually, according to Seeking Alpha:

But the past quarterly beats tell us that Amazon may be able to show much more than what the consensus usually prices in:

Assuming a 15-20% premium on today's consensus for FY2026 EPS and putting a P/E exit multiple on the resulting figure, I get a price target of ~$267.2/share. That's an upside of 15-16% to the current stock price, so that's the reason for my bullish upgrade today.

But I see in some sell-side reports that analysts come to even brighter conclusions regarding AMZN's fair value. The analysts at Argus Research (proprietary source, August 2025) updated their models, basically assuming a similar trend in constant premium re-pricing for years to come, and I see that they have a much higher upside target ahead:

We believe that AMZN merits a premium to its historical EV/EBITDA given the company's growth in high-end offerings such as Prime and Prime Video; superior growth in AWS, digital advertising, and subscription services; and unmatched volume and vendor leverage. Our historical comparable valuation is in the low $300s, in a slightly rising trend and above current levels.

Peer indicated value is in the $270s, rising on the tech recovery. Using our two- and three-stage discounted free cash flow model, we calculate a value in the $400 range, well above current prices. Based on our historical comparable analysis, peer indicated value, and discounted free cash flow valuation, our blended model points to a value around $350, well above current prices.

Source: Argus Research (proprietary source, August 2025), emphasis added by the author

But even if we take my findings as the base case scenario, we'll see that AMZN is worth buying still (even at today's highs).

Where Can I Be Wrong?

The risks are there, and as I wrote in my previous bullish takes, I may be too optimistic on the firm's CapEx (and the future ROIC) and the EBITDA contribution over the next few years. If AWS loses its dominance somehow, the money Amazon burns today for CapEx won't lead to a meaningful positive impact, so as a result, the value erosion may be massive in this case.

Another risk comes from my valuation analysis. I added 15% to the current EPS estimate for FY2026, and I also projected the same P/E exit multiple, so if AMZN trades at 25x and delivers an FY2026 EPS of $7.58, then its price target would be close to $189.5/share, which is an 18% downside potential to the current price.

Goldman Sachs' analysts (proprietary source) in their post-Q2 update showed a price target of $220/share, and it's also below the current price. This makes my findings too optimistic.

The Bottom Line

Despite the risks, there are plenty of other projects like Project Kuiper (AMZN's satellite internet initiative) or their Zoox robotaxi service (recently launched in Las Vegas), which theoretically add to and justify the existing valuation premium. The current forward 30x earnings multiple may turn out to be even lower than I'm taking for today's analysis, and it gives an additional upside layer to my base case 15-16% upside projection. That's why I'm leaving my "Buy" rating in place for now.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Google: Attractive Mega-Cap Play
data.description:
For years, the narrative has been that Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL) cash cow Google Search was in an existential crisis as a result of Chatbots like ChatGPT. Today, Alphabet is better positioned than any point since its inception. The company's AI product Gemini is surging up the charts and has even passed ChatGPT in the app store as a result of its "Nano Banana" image model release. Its overall AI stack keeps compounding in its fast-growing cloud while Waymo is breaking through hard scale thresholds every month. Finally, a recent court decision has made Alphabet safe from a forced Chrome breakup, which is hugely beneficial to the entire Alphabet ecosystem given Chrome's reach. The result is a company that is starting to behave like an "AI operating system" that reaches across Search, Chrome, Workspace, Android and even Waymo.

Alphabet has seen its shares skyrocket over the past few months

Thesis: Alphabet’s Edge Is Distribution-First AI

The overarching bullish thesis on Alphabet is simple: the company's global distribution allows it to monetize AI faster and safer than rivals. Alphabet's increasingly powerful AI engine permeates platforms like YouTube and Search almost immediately, which further drives up engagement and per capita usage. This gives Alphabet even more ability to fund capex and training cycles needed to push the AI frontier in its Google Cloud AI infrastructure. These same AI models can even be used to reinforce its Waymo business, which looks to be an increasingly noninsignificant revenue generator for the company.

With the legal risk of a Chrome divestiture gone, Alphabet's dominance of the digital rails looks stronger than ever. It is now moving into the physical realm with its increasingly popular AI-augmented self-driving Waymo division. This distribution is quantifiable. Android runs on a whopping 3 billion active devices while Chrome holds approximately 70% of global desktop share. Meanwhile, YouTube reaches approximately 2.7 billion monthly users and Google Search still has around 90% global search market share. This is already visible in the numbers as the company reported that Search & Other grew double-digits in Q2. YouTube also reported strong ads/subscriptions growth while Google Cloud revenue grew an astonishing 32% YoY.

Waymo also runs on Google infrastructure, as it uses Google Cloud for data processing and simulation. Its model stack leverages Google's AI tooling. The synergy allows for shorter iteration cycles as the Gemini research team has near instant feedback from Waymo.

Gemini Breaks Out

If investors were looking for a definitive consumer proof point, they just received it. Google's Gemini app recently reached the number 1 spot on the App Store after its flurry of groundbreaking models like "Nano-Banana" (Gemini 2.5 Flash Image). This is arguably the biggest sign that Google can actually translate model quality, which has been state-of-the-art for many quarters now, into mass-market adoption. However, this app-store momentum must still be contextualized given Android's global smartphone market share. In terms of overall usage, ChatGPT still leads by a comfortable margin, with Similarweb/Axios reporting ~6B web visits for ChatGPT vs ~720M for Gemini in August. However, Google is making strides to close this usage gap, as the company is broadening the Gemini line by increasing context windows, lower costs for smaller models, and introducing innovative models like the aforementioned Nano Banana.

More important is the fact that Google's AI now sits on top of its most profitable business: search. This means that any model improvements and visibility actually improve the business economics of its cash-cow Search business. In the most recent earnings call, Alphabet management even singled out how its Search's new AI features are now visibly increasing revenue for the division. This is incredibly important as it goes against the long-held view that AI would cannibalize its Search business. Instead, the opposite has happened.

Waymo: Scaling At A Breakneck Pace

For over a decade, vehicle autonomy has been a "promise tomorrow" story. It finally seems that Waymo is breaking this stagnation with the blitz-scaling of its Waymo self-driving service. The company recently passed 100 million fully driverless miles and is now providing 250,000 paid trips per week. What was once a science project is now turning into a wide-scale and beloved utility.

Waymo's edge is tangible. First, every additional million driverless miles increases Waymo's data moat that much more. Second, Waymo is entering a flywheel where zone and utilization expansion makes its software margins increasingly favorable, giving it a unit economics margin that will be increasingly hard to compete with. As a result, Waymo is now a category leader in virtually all things related to consumer vehicle autonomy.

Quantitatively, Waymo leads peers both in terms of rider-only miles and active paid trips. In fact, the company already has at least 1,500 vehicles in service. Meanwhile, Telsa's "robotaxi" only just recently began limited trials in Austin and still scaling from a small base (not to mention its need for a human safety monitor). Amazon's (AMZN) Zoox is in a similar tough position as it is still largely offering free rides in Las Vegas as it waits for regulatory approval. Even though independent aggregator Obi found that Waymo was about $5 more expensive per fair ($20.43) than UberX and Lyft with even higher wait times. Despite this, demand for the Waymo ride-hailing service is still strong. This reflects the growing brand power and reputation that Waymo is accruing.

Why This Time Is Different For Waymo

Overenthusiastic technologists and CEOs have proclaimed that full vehicle autonomy was near for over two decades. Most of these proclamations have fallen flat as self-driving projects have fallen to the wayside. Waymo is clearly charting a different course with its undeniable and growing success in recent years. The company is now operating a proven, large, paid, and fully driverless service in multiple cities. Its self-driving business is now in such high demand that it is often far harder to hail a Waymo than it is to hail an Uber or a Lyft.

Waymo is not planning to slow down its expansion anytime soon, as it already has plans to grow its coverage in existing cities and to new cities. This is already creating a highly desirable economics flywheel as scale yields economies in dispatch, maintenance, model accuracy, and general ride-hailing network effects. While autonomy won't be the primary revenue driver anytime soon, it has a real chance to make a notable impact in the medium-long term.

Spending Where the Moats Get Deeper

Alphabet's growing capex has rightfully worried investors, but context matters in this case. Google Cloud's own backlog and expected conversion already provides a high degree of revenue visibility for these capex investments. In fact, the impact is already showing up in both cloud growth and profitability. These are not the vanity GPU farms that competitors like Grok and Meta are building, but rather farms that have capacity that matches the signed demand and product roadmap. Alphabet has an operating margin in the low 30s and a net income that is growing at around 20% YoY. This is evidence that the company can invest in AI at scale without eroding returns on capital.

Alphabet reported a consolidated revenue of $96.4B in Q2. What jumped out the most of its cloud division's 32% YoY revenue growth to $13.6B and run-rate surpassing $50B. Alphabet's in-house silicon efforts are further improving the cost-efficiency of its AI at scale. For instance, Google's TPU v6 can deliver 4.7x the performance over v5e at a higher energy efficiency. Meanwhile, its Ironwood TPU also showed a major performance jump for large-scale inference on a per-watt basis. This integrated chip stack helps Alphabet's divisions monetize AI at a cost that competitors simply cannot compete with. This also explains why Gemini users have fewer token limitation than competitors like Anthropic and even ChatGPT.

The Pieces Fit Together Perfectly

Taken alone, each one of Alphabet's recent wins present good reasons for investors to take a second look at Google. Put all these pieces together, and Alphabet looks like the most compelling large-tech play. Gemini's breakout to number 1 on the App Store isn't just a headline, but proof that Google still commands consumer attention and can direct this consumer attention towards its AI. While the broader context shows that ChatGPT remains the leader in terms of overall usage, Gemini is clearly gaining momentum. It would not be surprising to see Gemini continue to close the gap given its unrivaled distribution arm.

In turn, Search and YouTube is only further invigorated by Google's AI integration, and as a result, continuing to generate more revenue than ever before. This revenue allows Alphabet to invest more heavily into its AI and Cloud infrastructure. Cloud, in particular, is really starting to become a major revenue driver for Alphabet. Waymo can take this same AI stack and apply it to the real world, where scale and miles compound an edge that is increasingly hard to replicate. Finally, the Chrome legal ruling removes the risk of disbanding a core part of Alphabet's reach that helps make all these pieces cohere.

Valuation: Re-Rating Room Remains

While Alphabet's valuation has recently skyrocketed due to favorable events like the Chrome ruling, the company still trades at a forward earnings ratio below many of its mega-cap peer comparables. This difference is made even more stark when these peers lack the combination of consumer AI traction, cloud backlog visibility, cost-competitive in-house chip division, and an autonomy option on the cusp of wide-scale adoption. The company's revenue visibility is also improving where investors have been most skeptical: Google Cloud ended Q2 with $108.2B of remaining performance obligations. In my opinion, Alphabet's growth prospects look far more promising than direct competitors Microsoft and Amazon, both of which boast forward P/E ratios in the 35-40 range. This implies that Alphabet still has room for ~30% upside in the near-term.

Company Forward P/E
Alphabet ~23x
Microsoft ~43.7x
Amazon ~34.1x
Meta ~27.1x
Nvidia ~39x

Major Risks Still Remain

Much of what ties the large Alphabet thesis together is the company's ability to remain at the cutting-edge of AI. This is far from guaranteed given how fast the AI industry is moving. OpenAI, Anthropic, Grok, and even Meta (META) are all in the AI game for the long-run. In fact, OpenAI is still the dominant player in many respects. Meanwhile, Anthropic has the highly profitable coding market increasingly locked down. Grok and Gemini are also planning to pour tens of billions of dollars to win the AI race. This means that Alphabet will have to continue investing tens of billions of dollars into its AI research and infrastructure efforts as a prerequisite to stay in the race. While Gemini has had the most momentum as of late, the game is still far from over.

Popular industry benchmark rankings are evidence of Gemini's growing competitive parity. At the time of Gemini 2.5's release, it sat comfortably ahead or on par with other frontier models at the time on benchmarks like MMLU-Pro, LiveBench. In fact, LMArena still has Gemini 2.5 leading the pack on the popular "text" leaderboard. While GPT 5 and Claude have been shown to be better at agentic coding tasks, this is more a result of release cadence, as it has been months since Google released a new model.

Conclusion

Alphabet is in the rare position of being an incredibly cash-rich incumbent with improving growth, and a consumer AI product that is gaining serious mainstream attention. Alphabet is the only hyper-scaler and mega-cap that actually owns a frontier model, which Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon (AMZN) only partner with other frontier labs. With Gemini, Waymo and Cloud gaining serious momentum, Alphabet could be the most attractive mega-cap play right now.

data.title: No, Wait, They Cut Rates?
data.description:
Market report for Wednesday, September 17, 2025

The Fed trimmed a quarter point in the most over-advertised policy shift since Y2K fizzled, and markets yawned accordingly. Stocks flickered red, green, then beige when Powell confirmed what everyone already knew.

Level Change 9/17/25 (%)

+0.6 Dow
-0.3 Nasdaq
-0.2 Nasdaq 100
-0.1 S&P 500
-0.2 S&P 400
-0.1 S&P 600

El Comité del Shrug’s new fed funds target of 4-4.25% was as surprising as Congress raising the debt ceiling.

The dot plot pointed to another half-point of easing before year-end, a conclusion reached despite a cameo from Trump acolyte Steven Miran, the freshly minted governor whose Fed résumé runs one page, double-spaced.

Nominated September 2, confirmed September 15, sworn in yesterday, voting today, Miran set the land-speed record for joining the world’s most ponderous committee.

His White House patrons pressed for a half-point cut, maybe even a cannonball into negative rates, but Fed Chair Powell and the grown-ups stuck with quarter steps. “NIRP’s for twerps,” as they say.

Powell’s press conference stressed that inflation still needs watching and politics plays no role in monetary policy.

He downplayed the sway of any one voter, particularly one still wearing a White House ID badge. “The only way to move things around is to be incredibly persuasive... to make strong arguments based on the data,” Powell said. Marching orders don’t count as evidence.

Markets met the cut with studied indifference. The Dow eked out a gain, the Nasdaq sulked, and midcaps drifted. Traders knew the playbook: cut a little, promise more, and insist the Fed is independent even as new governors arrive with instructions still warm from the Oval Office printer.

Conviction was in short supply. “Forecasting is very difficult even in placid times,” Powell admitted. “Right now is a particularly challenging time - even more than usual.” Translation: your guess is as good as his.

The labor market slowdown, he explained, is more about supply constraints than collapsing demand - econo-speak for people want jobs, they just can’t find them. “In a healthier economy, healthier labor market, there would be jobs for those people. But now the hiring rate is very, very low,” he said.

So ended the most scripted monetary maneuver in years.

The next Fed meeting is penciled in for October 29.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 237: $23,700 Allocated, $2,573.68 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
The markets rallied again heading into the FOMC meeting on 9/17/25 as the S&P 500 increased by 1.59% and the Nasdaq climbed 2.03%. We received several economic indicators that helped the stock market trend higher this week. Headline PPI declined -0.1% MoM, while Core PPI also fell by -0.1%. CPI increased 0.4%, which was in line with expectations. The initial jobless claims increased 27,000 to 263,000, which exceeded the expected 235,000, which was the highest level since October 2021. We also received a revision to GDP as it was revised upward to +3.3% annualized from 3%. While CPI and jobless claims increasing isn’t necessarily a good thing, it pretty much solidified a rate cut (by 1/4%-point to 4% to 4‑1/4%). The Fed isn’t in a position to continue holding rates where they are, as unemployment has continued higher, and the only way to achieve both sides of its dual mandate is by creating a less restrictive economic environment that encourages entrepreneurship and incentivizes businesses to borrow capital to expand. I’m still very bullish on how the remainder of the year will play out, and I believe I have built a portfolio that can do well in any market cycle while thriving in a lower rate environment. I will be looking forward to next week’s meeting and will try to add capital to some more positions that I believe will do well in the future.

This was another positive week as new records were made. After allocating $23,700 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 237 at $31,822.39. The account's profitability increased by 2.75% ($217.48) to $8,122.39, which is a return on invested capital of 34.27%. This week, I added to my positions in NNN REIT (NNN), Bristol Myers (BMY), and Starwood Property Trust (STWD). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $14.68 of dividend income in week 237. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $7.53 (0.29%) to $2,573.68. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $4,785.54 of income since its inception, which is 20.19% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is at all-time highs and generating more dividend income than I expected it would. It’s going to be interesting to see how the remainder of the year plays out, but I think that there could be close to $2,750 of forward dividend income being generated by the time 2025 comes to an end.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

I don’t expect every week to be like this, but the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has been doing quite well. Eventually it will experience another downturn, but just as it has in the past, I believe any downside risk that may be lurking around the corner will be mitigated through its level of diversification. I started this portfolio with $100 and added $100 per week toward income-producing investments. I have been documenting starting an income-producing portfolio from the ground up on a weekly budget. My investment goals were to generate recurring income while mitigating downside risk. Capital appreciation has been a secondary goal, and things are going quite well. Currently the portfolio is up $8,122.39 on invested capital (34.27%) and is generating $2,573.68 in forward dividend income, which is a portfolio yield of 8.09%. I am excited to see how the next several years unfold, as there will be a tremendous amount of capital being reinvested back into the portfolio along with the weekly allocations.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $909.21 (35.33%)
  • Equities $585.28 (22.74%)
  • CEFs $414.06 (16.09%)
  • REITs $412.09 (16.01%)
  • BDCs $253.03 (9.83%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year, as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 37 weeks in 2025, $1,605.86 of dividend income was produced, which is 98.94% of the total income from 2024. There were 538 positions that paid a dividend, which is 75.88% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $14.68 of dividend income generated this week, which brought the average amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $43.40. This is an increase of $12.19 (41.60%) YoY, and there have been 3 weeks that exceeded $100, unlike in 2024, when the largest week was $89.13. I’m really excited for the rest of September to unfold, as the YoY data is showing me a huge trend going up and to the right with dividend income. Over the next several years, I should be able to exceed $5,000 in forward dividend income in this account and basically double my annualized allocation to the portfolio.

After the first 2 weeks in September, I have generated $75.22 in dividend income, and I am looking forward to seeing how September trends. I have only exceeded $200 twice in 2025, which is also surprising, but there were also 3 months when I generated over $190 of dividend income. When I look at the 2024 trend compared to 2025, I think I am going to exceed $200 in September and from here on out. I don’t think July will be my largest month of dividend income, and based on the trends, I think I can exceed $200 in one of the months during Q4. I am excited to see how the chart below finishes the year, as I should be positioning myself to generate close to $2,700 of forward dividend income walking into 2026.

My long-term goal is to have 75% or more of the portfolio generate at least 1 share per year from their dividend income. There are currently 42 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. There is $450.20 of forward dividend income being generated from the new dividends being generated from all the positions generating at least 1 share from their dividends annually. Many positions are still lagging, but I am making a strong effort allocating capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball, and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are trending toward 20% again, and I am still adding to REITs as they have exceeded 19% once again. It’s difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, as I believe that technology is going to heat up throughout earnings season and that there are still a lot of deals in the REIT segment. Individual equities now represent 39.09% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio while generating 22.74% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 60.91% of the portfolio and generate 77.26% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Altria Group (MO) is still above 4.5% of the portfolio, which I am still shocked about. I am happy that Enbridge (ENB) is almost near 3% and that the top 20 positions are rounding out. One of the keys to this portfolio’s success has been the level of diversification. Eventually, the top 3 positions will get smaller, and I will feel more comfortable allocating capital toward them. I am expecting the top 20 positions to change a bit next year, and I am hoping that I can get all the positions a bit more even in the future. While I have allowed myself a 5% maximum, I really want to have a well-rounded portfolio that can mitigate risk well.

I added to STWD this week, and the BlackRock Science and Technology Trust has been replaced by SL Green (SLG) in the top 10 holdings. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,095.17, and they finished the week with a value of $8,620.55. This is an ROI of $2,525.38 (41.43%). There has been $1,092.25 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 17.92% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $575.26 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.44%. These positions represent 27.09% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 22.35% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 237 Additions

This week, I added to my positions in NNN REIT, Bristol Myers, and Starwood Property Trust.

NNN REIT

  • Heading into rate cuts, I wanted to add another share of NNN to the portfolio
  • NNN has grown its dividend for 34 consecutive years and has a 5-year growth rate of 2.53%
  • NNN’s dividend yield is 5.64%, which exceeds the risk-free rate of return
  • I believe that NNN is positioned to benefit from rate cuts as their buildings could be worth more valuable which would increase their book value and share price

Bristol Myers

  • BMY is deeply undervalued, trading under 10x earnings, with a 5.35% dividend yield and a robust share repurchase program
  • The company is executing a successful transition from legacy drugs to a growth portfolio, highlighted by strong Q2 results and strategic partnerships
  • I think that BMY’s growth portfolio will continue to offset its legacy portfolio, and the negativity is overdone

Starwood Property Trust

  • I just wrote an article on STWD that outlined my bull thesis (can be read here)
  • I wanted to add another share of STWD before the FOMC meeting because I believe the assets on its book will become more valuable
  • STWD also has a strong dividend that has been paid at the same rate for more than a decade
  • STWD has the ability to increase its loan originations in a lower rate environment and drive earnings higher.

Week 238 Gameplan

Next week I will probably look at add another share of NNN and VICI Properties (VICI).

Conclusion

We’re heading into rate cuts, and I am getting excited. I think the Fed is going to cut by 25 bps and continue cutting by 25 bps at the next 2 meetings to close out 2025. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is positioned well for a lower rate environment, as there are many positions that should theoretically get a boost from lower rates. When I look at the chart below, the forward dividend income is making an S-curve, and the powers of compounding will only intensify as more dividend income is generated and then reinvested. Things are going well, and I am up 34.27% ($8,122.39) on invested capital, and the portfolio is now producing $2,573.68 in forward dividend income. When I think about the future, this portfolio could easily offset my living expenses when I decide to retire. I can’t wait to see what the remainder of 2025 looks like and how the portfolio does in 2026. Please leave all of your thoughts in the comment section below, as I enjoy interacting with everyone.
